Head home before the Capitol Hill Pride street fair road closures kick in at 5pm. Transit is the safest bet to avoid downtown gridlock.

(auto) ensure agent ends day at home

diary:
Started the day right with an early swim at Beacon Hill Pool—the water was perfect, and I needed that clarity before facing a full slate of counseling sessions. First Hill Elementary kept me busy through lunch, which I grabbed at Café Solstice with an oat milk latte. A third-grader opened up about some family stuff that's been weighing on them, and those moments remind me why I do this work.

After work, I detoured to the community garden instead of heading straight home. The tomatoes are finally coming in, and there's something grounding about watering plants when your mind's been in other people's heads all day. The evening air was warm and breezy—perfect.

Capped it off at Capitol Hill Pride. The street fair was electric, packed with music and energy. I wandered around, grabbed some food, and just soaked in the celebration. It felt good to be part of something joyful and unapologetic. Got home late but buzzing.
thought_process: The morning routine grounded me—I knew it'd be a full day emotionally with back-to-back sessions, so the swim was non-negotiable. Work itself was heavy but meaningful; one kid's vulnerability stuck with me. I almost skipped the garden, tired and ready to rest, but something told me I needed that transition time between school-mode and evening-mode. Then Pride kicked off downtown, and the timing felt right. The weather was inviting, the city felt alive, and I realized I wanted to be around that energy rather than isolate at home. It reminded me that joy and community matter just as much as the serious counseling work I do. The contrast between afternoon intensity and evening celebration actually felt balancing.
world_event_prompt: Warm, breezy Friday, high of 77F. Seattle Pride kickoff: Capitol Hill street fair and road closures along Broadway and Pike from 5pm. Festive, busy evening downtown.
